---
title: "Furrballs: A NUMA-Aware, High-Performance Caching Library"
subtitle: "Technical Report v0.7"
author: "The Sphynx"
date: last-modified
lang: en
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    documentclass: scrartcl
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - top=1in
      - bottom=1in
      - left=1in
      - right=1in
    linestretch: 1.12
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{microtype}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{caption}
        \captionsetup{font=small,labelfont=bf}
        \usepackage[automark]{scrlayer-scrpage}
        \clearpairofpagestyles
        \ihead{Furrballs Technical Report}
        \ohead{\pagemark}
        \KOMAoptions{headsepline=true}
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

## Status
Draft v0.7 (living document)

## Abstract
Furrballs is a NUMA-aware caching library that uses NUMA topology as a first-class input to cache placement and eviction decisions. The core contribution is per-page allocation on specific NUMA nodes, with a two-level ARC hierarchy: key-level ARC (L1) and page-level ARC (L2), backed by RocksDB persistence (L3). Pages are pre-allocated at initialization via one physical allocation per NUMA node, subdivided into logical pages filled by a bump allocator. Key routing supports both round-robin distribution and thread-local placement (via a runtime config flag). The library is implemented in C++20, uses a platform abstraction layer (Numatic) for NUMA operations, and integrates a custom synchronization library (StreamLine) for thread coordination. Lock-free reads are implemented via SeqLock on per-node sharded KeyMeta, eliminating shared_mutex overhead from the read path. Benchmark results on a QEMU NUMA simulation (2 nodes, 6 vCPUs) demonstrate that lock-free reads expose an 11.7% p50 and 13.3% p99 cross-node overhead---more than double the signal visible under shared_mutex (5.1% p50). Thread-local routing achieves 26-30% p50 and 31-41% p99 improvement over round-robin with lock-free reads, confirming that NUMA-aware placement provides compound benefits in both memory locality and synchronization reduction. A cross-VM baseline comparison (Furrballs in a 2-node NUMA VM vs a functionally identical non-NUMA cache in a separate 1-node VM) reveals that per-node sharding with hash-based routing provides a 3x concurrent Set and 1.25x concurrent Get throughput advantage through natural lock partitioning, at a modest 4-6% single-threaded cost. QEMU does not simulate real cross-node memory latency, so real hardware validation is needed to measure the true net effect.

## 1. Problem Statement

Modern multi-socket servers expose NUMA topology where memory access latency depends on which socket the data resides on. Remote NUMA access (cross-socket) can be 1.2-2x slower than local access. Existing caching systems either ignore NUMA topology entirely (Redis, Memcached) or handle it at coarse granularity (Meta's CacheLib uses static per-node sharding). No published system combines NUMA topology as an adaptive input to per-page cache placement decisions.

Furrballs targets this gap: allocate cache pages on the NUMA node of the thread that accesses them, measure the locality benefit, and provide a structured architecture for evolving the policy.

## 2. Design

### 2.1 Architecture Overview

```
L1: ARC<string, KeyMeta>     -- Key-level cache, tracks hot keys
L2: ARC<PageIndex, Page*>  -- Page-level cache, tracks hot pages (Phase 2)
L3: RocksDB                 -- Persistent storage, written on eviction/shutdown
```

Phase 1 (current): L1 ARC + pre-allocated pages + RocksDB write-on-shutdown. Pages are never evicted from memory.

### 2.2 Memory Allocation

At initialization (`CreateBall`), one physical allocation is made per NUMA node via `Numatic::AllocateOnNode()`. This block is subdivided into logical pages (each `PageSize` bytes). All logical pages are pre-populated in cache.

```cpp
// Per node:
physicalBlock = Numatic::AllocateOnNode(physicalPageSize, nodeId)
for i in 0..numPages:
    page[i].Data = (char*)physicalBlock + i * config.PageSize
    page[i].NodeId = nodeId
```

Container allocations within nodes use `std::pmr::memory_resource` backed by `NumaLocalMemoryResource`, ensuring vector backing arrays land on the correct node.

### 2.3 Key-Based API

```cpp
Error Set(const std::string& key, void* data, size_t size);
Error Get(const std::string& key, void* outBuf, size_t bufSize, size_t& outSize);
```

Key routing supports two strategies: round-robin distribution via `AtomicRoundRobin` (v0.5), thread-local placement via `Numatic::GetCurrentNode()` (v0.5-v0.6), and hash-based deterministic routing via `std::hash<std::string>{}(key) % nodeCount` (v0.7, current). Hash-based routing was adopted because it eliminates the need for `Get()` to iterate all node shards: both `Set()` and `Get()` compute the target node from the key hash, enabling a single-shard lookup. The trade-off is the loss of thread-local placement flexibility---the hash decides the node, not the calling thread's locality.

### 2.4 Bump Allocator

Multi-value pages use an append-only bump allocator with 8-byte alignment. Each page tracks `UsedBytes` (atomic, CAS-based concurrent bump) and `DataWastedByPadding` for fragmentation metrics. No per-value deallocation -- pages are evicted wholesale by ARC (Phase 2).

### 2.5 Concurrency

Per-node `shared_mutex` serializes writes (map insert/update + page bump). Reads are lock-free via `SeqLock<KeyMeta>`: `Get()` computes the target node from `std::hash<std::string>{}(key) % nodeCount`, does a single `unordered_map::find()` on that node's shard, calls `SeqLock::Read()` on matching entries, and copies data without taking any lock. The SeqLock sequence counter provides deterministic consistency---if a writer is active (odd sequence), the reader retries. Statistics use `alignas(64)` on all atomic fields to prevent false sharing.

### 2.6 Platform Abstraction (Numatic)

All NUMA operations are isolated behind stateless free functions:

| Category | Functions |
|----------|----------|
| Topology | `IsNUMAAvailable`, `GetNodeCount`, `GetDistance` |
| Threading | `PinCurrentThreadToNode`, `GetCurrentNode` |
| Allocation | `AllocateOnNode`, `AllocateLocal`, `FreeNUMA` |
| Huge Pages | `AllocateOnNodeHuge`, `AllocateLocalHuge`, `IsHugePagesAvailable`, `GetHugePageSize` |
| Migration | `MovePages`, `MigratePages`, `GetPageNode` |
| PMR | `NumaLocalMemoryResource`, `NumaNodeMemoryResource` (CRTP base) |

Linux implementation uses libnuma. Windows has stubs/heuristics for functions without direct equivalents.

## 3. Design Decisions Log

### 3.1 Per-Node Physical Block with Subdivision
**Decision:** One `Numatic::AllocateOnNode()` call per NUMA node at init, manually partitioned into logical pages.

**Rationale:** Avoids syscall overhead per page. Single allocation + pointer arithmetic is faster than N `numa_alloc_onnode` calls. AMP (Phase 3) will handle expansion to multiple physical blocks.

### 3.2 PMR-Backed Per-Node Containers
**Decision:** `PerNodeDetails::NodePages` uses `std::pmr::vector<Page>` backed by `NumaLocalMemoryResource`.

**Rationale:** Standard `std::vector` allocates on the OS's preferred node. PMR with a NUMA-local resource ensures the backing array memory lands on the correct node, since `PerNodeDetails` is constructed on a pinned worker thread.

### 3.3 Key Routing Strategy
**Decision:** Keys are routed via `std::hash<std::string>{}(key) % nodeCount`, deterministically placing each key on a specific node. Both `Set()` and `Get()` compute the same hash, enabling single-shard lookup without a routing table.

**Rationale:** Previous versions used round-robin (`AtomicRoundRobin`) and thread-local (`Numatic::GetCurrentNode()`) routing. Both required `Get()` to iterate all node shards, causing a 6-8x concurrent throughput penalty (later found to be partially a benchmark artifact). Hash-based routing eliminates shard iteration entirely---`Get()` checks exactly one shard, reducing the NUMA awareness overhead to 10-15% for concurrent reads vs a non-NUMA baseline. The trade-off is losing thread-local placement flexibility: the hash decides the node, not the calling thread's locality. For read-heavy workloads where `Get()` dominates, this trade-off is favorable.

### 3.4 All-or-Nothing NUMA Init
**Decision:** If any node fails to allocate during `CreateBall()`, all successful allocations are freed and `nullptr` is returned.

**Rationale:** Partial NUMA topology (e.g., one node fails, fallback to single-node) eliminates the NUMA variable from benchmarks. One node = uniform memory access = no NUMA effect to measure.

### 3.5 WaitGroup for Parallel Init
**Decision:** NodeJob workers allocate their node's physical block in parallel, synchronized by StreamLine's `WaitGroup`.

**Rationale:** Init is blocking by design (the FurrBall must be fully constructed or not at all). WaitGroup provides zero-overhead thread synchronization without busy-waiting.

### 3.6 Error Codes, Not Exceptions
**Decision:** Public API returns `Error` enum. Exceptions only for unrecoverable programmer errors (WaitGroup misuse).

**Rationale:** Consistent with systems programming conventions. The `Error` enum is categorized (general, initialization, memory, cache, storage, compression, threading, I/O) for easy filtering.

### 3.7 `alignas(64)` on Statistics Atomics
**Decision:** Each atomic in `Statistics` sits on its own 64-byte cache line.

**Rationale:** Prevents false sharing between concurrent `HitCount`, `MissCount`, `BytesWritten`, and `BytesRead` updates in multi-threaded benchmarks.

## 4. Related Work

| System | NUMA Approach | Difference from Furrballs |
|---|---|---|
| TCMalloc | NUMA-aware allocation | Allocator only; no cache eviction or page management |
| jemalloc | NUMA-aware arenas | Allocator only; no cache policy |
| CacheLib (Meta) | Static sharding per node | Coarse-grained; entire cache instance per node |
| PostgreSQL | Shared buffers with partition locks | Database buffer pool; not a standalone cache |
| InnoDB | Buffer pool instances | Database storage; not a cache library |
| Redis | No NUMA awareness | Baseline comparison point |
| Furrballs | Per-page placement + two-level ARC | Fine-grained, adaptive, page-level NUMA locality as policy input |

No published work addresses the specific intersection of NUMA topology and adaptive cache eviction policy.

## 5. Evaluation

### 5.1 Methodology

Benchmarks run on two platforms:
- **Host (baseline):** Single NUMA node, VPS, Ubuntu 24.04, x86_64, GCC 14, CMake Release
- **QEMU VM (NUMA simulation):** 2 NUMA nodes, 6 vCPUs (3 per node), 4GB RAM, Ubuntu 24.04

Each scenario is repeated 5 times. Reported values are averages across iterations. The QEMU VM adds approximately 600ns of emulation overhead per operation. To isolate the NUMA effect, we compare local vs cross-node reads under identical conditions within the VM.

### 5.2 Single-Threaded Baseline (Host, 1 NUMA Node, 5 iterations averaged)

| Value Size | Operation | Throughput (ops/s) | p50 (ns) | p99 (ns) | Stddev (ns) |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------:|--------:|--------:|-----------:|
| 64B | Set | 4,333,091 | 146 | 830 | 1,060 |
| 64B | Get | 4,473,004 | 134 | 255 | 1,394 |
| 512B | Set | 3,753,818 | 171 | 1,433 | 1,332 |
| 512B | Get | 4,735,494 | 147 | 245 | 193 |
| 4KB | Set | 1,660,059 | 926 | 2,686 | 1,167 |
| 4KB | Get | 2,888,215 | 270 | 442 | 241 |

### 5.3 Single-Threaded (QEMU VM, 2 NUMA Nodes, 5 iterations averaged)

| Value Size | Operation | Throughput (ops/s) | p50 (ns) | p99 (ns) | Stddev (ns) |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------:|--------:|--------:|-----------:|
| 64B | Set | 679,054 | 695 | 2,674 | 3,416 |
| 64B | Get | 766,818 | 655 | 915 | 820 |
| 512B | Set | 581,262 | 760 | 3,249 | 7,293 |
| 512B | Get | 757,711 | 672 | 985 | 918 |
| 4KB | Set | 499,495 | 1,561 | 6,170 | 2,170 |
| 4KB | Get | 677,780 | 799 | 1,048 | 1,256 |

Host-to-VM ratio: approximately 5-6x slower in the VM due to QEMU emulation overhead.

### 5.4 NUMA Effect (QEMU VM, 2 threads, 5 iterations averaged)

Two threads, each pinned to a different NUMA node. One thread writes keys to its local node, the other reads them from the remote node. Results compare shared_mutex reads (v0.5) vs SeqLock lock-free reads (v0.6).

| Value Size | Metric | Local (ns) | Cross-Node (ns) | Overhead |
|-----------|--------|----------:|---------------:|--------:|
| 64B (v0.5, shared_mutex) | p50 | 956 | 1,005 | 5.1% |
| 64B (v0.5, shared_mutex) | p99 | 1,869 | 2,075 | 11.0% |
| 64B (v0.6, SeqLock) | p50 | 1,157 | 1,292 | **11.7%** |
| 64B (v0.6, SeqLock) | p99 | 2,093 | 2,372 | **13.3%** |
| 512B (v0.6, SeqLock) | p50 | 1,252 | 1,299 | 3.8% |
| 512B (v0.6, SeqLock) | p99 | 2,199 | 2,243 | 2.0% |

**Key finding:** Lock-free reads (SeqLock) expose a cross-node p50 overhead of 11.7%, more than double the 5.1% visible under `shared_mutex`. The lock was masking the true NUMA memory latency signal. The p99 overhead increased from 11.0% to 13.3%, confirming the effect is consistent across latency percentiles. The 512-byte results remain noisy due to QEMU scheduling artifacts---real hardware validation is expected to show a cleaner signal at all value sizes.

This result validates the thesis methodology: synchronization overhead must be eliminated from the read path to measure the true NUMA effect.

### 5.5 Concurrent Throughput (QEMU VM, 4 threads, 5 iterations averaged)

Four threads, each pinned to a NUMA node, operating on disjoint key ranges. With SeqLock lock-free reads, cross-node Get throughput exceeds local due to reduced per-node contention.

| Value Size | Mode | Set (ops/s) | Get (ops/s) |
|-----------|------|-----------:|-----------:|
| 64B | All local (2 threads/node) | 761,315 | 1,197,488 |
| 64B | All cross-node | 952,933 | 1,592,695 |

Cross-node Get throughput is 33% higher than local under lock-free reads, suggesting that the QEMU NUMA simulation's memory access patterns interact favorably with the SeqLock retry loop when data resides on remote nodes. Real hardware validation is needed to confirm this behavior.

### 5.6 Routing Strategy Comparison (QEMU VM, 2 threads, 5 iterations averaged)

Two threads, each pinned to a different NUMA node (node 0 and node 1). Each thread writes 5000 keys (64 bytes), then reads its own keys (self-read) and the other thread's keys (cross-read). Results compare shared_mutex (v0.5) vs SeqLock lock-free reads (v0.6).

**v0.5 (shared_mutex reads):**

| Metric | Round-robin (ns) | Thread-local (ns) | Improvement |
|--------|-----------------:|-------------------:|------------:|
| Set p50 | 1,033 | 913 | **11.6%** |
| Set p99 | 11,288 | 5,503 | **51.2%** |
| Self-Get p50 | 907 | 835 | **8.0%** |
| Self-Get p99 | 2,096 | 1,357 | **35.3%** |
| Cross-Get p50 | 879 | 792 | **9.9%** |
| Cross-Get p99 | 1,953 | 1,358 | **30.5%** |

**v0.6 (SeqLock lock-free reads):**

| Metric | Round-robin (ns) | Thread-local (ns) | Improvement |
|--------|-----------------:|-------------------:|------------:|
| Set p50 | 1,621 | 1,355 | **16.4%** |
| Set p99 | 41,095 | 4,665 | **88.6%** |
| Self-Get p50 | 1,296 | 955 | **26.3%** |
| Self-Get p99 | 2,600 | 1,790 | **31.2%** |
| Cross-Get p50 | 1,292 | 901 | **30.3%** |
| Cross-Get p99 | 2,677 | 1,582 | **40.9%** |

Lock-free reads amplify the thread-local routing improvement from 8-10% p50 to **26-30% p50**. The lock was masking not only the NUMA effect but also the routing strategy benefit. Thread-local routing with SeqLock achieves 26-41% improvement across all Get metrics, confirming that the compound benefit of NUMA-aware placement (memory locality + reduced synchronization) is genuine and measurable.

### 5.7 Baseline Comparison: Cross-VM Isolation (3 runs each)

To isolate the NUMA contribution, Furrballs runs in the NUMA VM (2 nodes) and the BaselineCache runs in a separate non-NUMA VM (1 node, same QEMU overhead, no NUMA topology). The BaselineCache uses identical mechanisms (SeqLock reads, CAS bump allocator, per-op timing) but eliminates all NUMA-specific design choices: single `malloc`-backed allocation, single `unordered_map` (no per-node shards), single `shared_mutex`.

**Single-threaded 64B (3 runs averaged):**

| Implementation | Set p50 (ns) | Get p50 (ns) | Set ops/s | Get ops/s |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Furrballs (NUMA VM, hash routing) | 815 | 729 | 635K | 706K |
| Baseline (non-NUMA VM) | 783 | 687 | 612K | 687K |
| Delta | +4.1% | +6.1% | +3.8% | +2.8% |

**Concurrent 4-thread 64B (3 runs averaged, both with per-op timing):**

| Implementation | Set ops/s | Get ops/s |
|---|---:|---:|
| Furrballs (NUMA VM, hash routing) | 1,191K | 1,796K |
| Baseline (non-NUMA VM) | 390K | 1,438K |
| Ratio | **3.05x** | **1.25x** |

**Analysis:**

Furrballs' concurrent throughput *exceeds* the baseline in both Set (3x) and Get (1.25x). The mechanism is lock partitioning: Furrballs' per-node sharding creates N separate `shared_mutex` instances (one per NUMA node), so 4 threads contend on 2 mutexes instead of 1. Hash-based deterministic routing distributes keys evenly across shards, ensuring balanced contention. The baseline's single `shared_mutex` becomes the bottleneck under concurrent writes---all 4 threads serialize on one lock.

Single-threaded overhead is modest: 4-6% slower than the baseline, reflecting the cost of per-node allocation indirection and atomic statistics updates.

**Caveat:** QEMU does not simulate real cross-node memory latency. Remote memory access within the VM has identical latency to local access. On real hardware, the NUMA penalty (1.2-2x remote access overhead) would partially offset the lock partitioning benefit for read operations. The cross-VM comparison captures the architectural benefit (lock partitioning, smaller per-shard maps) without the hardware NUMA cost. Real hardware validation is needed to measure the true net effect.

Initial in-VM comparisons showed a 6-8x concurrent Get gap, which was traced to a benchmark methodology issue: Furrballs measured per-operation latency via `Clock::now()` (two VM exits per operation in QEMU), while the baseline measured only total wall time. With identical per-op timing and separate VMs, the gap reversed to a 1.25x advantage for Furrballs.

### 5.8 Limitations

- QEMU emulation adds noise to p50 latency measurements and does not simulate real cross-node memory access latency. The p99 tail metric is more reliable for NUMA effect detection within the VM. The cross-VM baseline comparison captures the architectural benefit (lock partitioning) without the hardware NUMA cost.
- 512-byte cross-node overhead remains noisy under QEMU. Requires real hardware validation.
- Lock-free reads are correct when no concurrent writes target the same shard (map `find()` is not thread-safe during concurrent `insert()`). The benchmark warmup-then-read pattern avoids this; production use requires a concurrent hash map.
- Cross-node reads in Phase 1 execute on the calling thread (synchronous). Phase 2 NodeJob dispatch will enable remote-node reads via pinned workers, potentially reducing cross-node overhead.
- Memory fragmentation from the bump allocator is not yet measured.
- The per-key `unique_ptr<SeqLock<KeyMeta>>` adds one heap allocation per key and one pointer indirection per read. A slab allocator for SeqLock entries would reduce this overhead.
- Per-node shard iteration in `Get()` imposes a 6-8x concurrent throughput penalty vs a non-NUMA baseline when benchmarked with unfair timing methodology. Hash-based deterministic routing (`hash(key) % numNodes`) eliminates shard iteration and reduces the gap to 10-15% for concurrent reads and 35% for concurrent writes.

## 6. Current Status

Phase 1 core is implemented and tested:
- L1 ARC with key-based Set/Get API (CAS bump allocator, round-robin + thread-local routing, per-node shards)
- Per-node physical block allocation with PMR-backed containers
- Lock-free reads via SeqLock on per-node sharded KeyMeta
- NodeJob worker pinning (one worker per NUMA node)
- WaitGroup-synchronized parallel init
- Full Numatic platform abstraction (13 functions, CRTP PMR allocators, Linux + Windows stubs)
- Benchmark harness (single-threaded, multi-threaded NUMA effect, concurrent throughput, routing comparison, 5 iterations with stddev)
- 7/7 unit tests passing

The project is on the `numa-focus` branch. Licensed under MIT. Build and test artifacts are excluded from version control.

## 7. Risks and Open Questions

- ~~Can lock-free reads (seqlock) reduce contention enough to expose NUMA effects at 4+ threads?~~ **Answered: yes.** SeqLock increased the measurable cross-node p50 overhead from 5.1% to 11.7%.
- Can the 11.7% p50 NUMA overhead measured on QEMU be replicated on real hardware with larger NUMA distances?
- Will Phase 2's L2 page eviction (ARC eviction) introduce latency spikes during page flush?
- How does the bump allocator's fragmentation ratio evolve under real workloads?
- Will the per-key `unique_ptr<SeqLock>` allocation overhead become a bottleneck at scale?

## 8. Roadmap

### Phase 1: NUMA-Aware Core (Current)
- [x] Per-node physical block allocation with manual subdivision
- [x] Bump allocator with padding waste tracking
- [x] Key-based Set/Get with round-robin + thread-local routing
- [x] Per-node sharded KeyStore (eliminates global map contention)
- [x] Lock-free reads via SeqLock on KeyMeta
- [x] CAS-based concurrent bump allocator
- [x] Statistics with cache-line-aligned atomics
- [x] Benchmark harness
- [x] Thread-local routing comparison (round-robin vs `Numatic::GetCurrentNode()`)
- [x] SeqLock isolation of NUMA memory latency signal
- [x] Baseline comparison (non-NUMA cache, concurrent throughput analysis)
- [x] Hash-based deterministic routing (eliminates shard iteration from read path)
- [ ] Full write-on-shutdown persistence verification
- [ ] Real hardware benchmark validation

### Phase 2: Two-Level ARC
- [ ] L2 ARC for page-level eviction
- [ ] Per-page key list for L2 eviction cleanup
- [ ] RocksDB page reload on L2 miss
- [ ] L1 ghost hit handling (key promoted, page still resident)

### Phase 3: Adaptive Memory Pooling (AMP)
- [ ] Dynamic page pool expansion under eviction pressure
- [ ] Page pool contraction when idle
- [ ] Multi-physical-block support per node

### Phase 4: Optimization
- [ ] O(1) ARC lookup (hash map + list iterator, replacing linear search)
- [ ] Concurrent hash map for per-node shards (safe lock-free reads during writes)
- [ ] SeqLock slab allocator (replace per-key unique_ptr)
- [ ] NodeJob async dispatch for Set/Get (remote-node reads)
- [ ] Reference counting on pages (dangling pointer safety for Phase 2 eviction)

### Phase 5: Publication
- [ ] Formal paper with reproducible benchmarks
- [ ] arXiv submission under `cs.DC`
- [ ] PhD application material

## 9. Conclusion

Furrballs demonstrates that NUMA topology can serve as a meaningful input to cache placement decisions. The architecture---per-node physical allocation, bump-packed multi-value pages, and two-level ARC hierarchy---provides a structured foundation for measuring and optimizing NUMA-aware caching. The critical finding is that synchronization overhead masks the true NUMA memory latency signal: under `shared_mutex`, cross-node overhead was 5.1% at p50; under SeqLock lock-free reads, it is 11.7%---more than double. Hash-based deterministic routing (`hash(key) % numNodes`) eliminates per-node shard iteration and, combined with per-node sharding, provides a 3x concurrent Set and 1.25x concurrent Get throughput advantage over a non-NUMA baseline through natural lock partitioning. Single-threaded overhead is modest at 4-6%. A cross-VM comparison initially appeared to show a 6-8x concurrent Get gap, which was traced to a benchmark methodology issue (per-operation `Clock::now()` calls causing VM exits, applied only to Furrballs). With fair timing and separate VMs, the gap reversed to a 1.25x advantage for Furrballs. QEMU does not simulate real cross-node memory latency, so the lock partitioning benefit is measured without the hardware NUMA cost. Real hardware validation is the remaining step before publication.

## Appendix A: Repository Structure
```
Furrballs/
  Furrballs/          # Core library (C++20, MIT license)
    include/
      Furrballs.h     # Public API, ARC, configs, KeyMeta
      Page.h          # Page struct with bump allocator
      Logger.h        # Thread-safe logging
      Error.h         # Categorized error enum
      NodeJob.h       # Per-node worker threading
      WaitGroup.h     # StreamLine synchronization (copied)
      Concept.h       # StreamLine Lockable concept (copied)
      SeqLock.h       # StreamLine SeqLock for lock-free reads (copied)
      MemoryManager.h # General allocation, padded_size utilities
    src/
      Furrballs.cpp   # Core: Bootstrap, CreateBall, Set, Get, Flush, Evict
      Logger.cpp      # Platform-safe time formatting
      NodeJob.cpp     # Worker loop and condition-variable dispatch
      Page.cpp        # (Empty; Page is header-only)
  numatic/            # NUMA platform abstraction
    include/Numatic.h  # Free function declarations + CRTP PMR allocators
    src/
      NumaticUnix.cpp  # Linux implementation (libnuma)
      NumaticWin.cpp   # Windows stubs/heuristics
  Benchmark/          # Benchmark harness
    BaselineCache.h   # Non-NUMA control group (malloc, single map, SeqLock)
    Benchmark.cpp     # Full benchmark suite (runs in NUMA VM)
    BaselineBenchmark.cpp  # Standalone baseline benchmark (runs in non-NUMA VM)
  Sandbox/            # Unit tests
  docs/               # This whitepaper
```

## Appendix B: Build and Run

```bash
mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release
cmake --build . --target Benchmark
./Benchmark/Benchmark
```

QEMU VM:
```bash
~/vm/furrballs/furr.sh benchmark  # Build on host, run in VM
```
